FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; baseline enrollment of 9,337 individuals with social media use and diffusion-weighted MRI data from ages 9-10 years.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional examination of social media consumption and white matter fractional anisotropy across 34 white matter tracts.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Biological Psychiatry (2025).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Identified phone/SM-specific variable 'social media consumption' (<30 min/day vs >30 min/day) paired with individual-trait neural outcome (fractional anisotropy of white matter tracts). Qualifies as SM variable paired with individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regressions examining effects of social media consumption on FA across 34 white matter tracts; reported β coefficients and p-values for each tract.

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric results throughout: odds ratios for demographic/contextual associations (OR > 1.5), linear regression estimates for FA associations. Results presented in text and supplementary materials.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; EC7 applies only to longitudinal studies where SM is DV-only. This study uses cross-sectional design with SM as IV.
notes: Associations between social media consumption and white matter FA became non-significant after adjustment for race and socioeconomic status. Study emphasizes importance of demographic and socioeconomic variables over SM use for white matter health in this age group.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study Release 4.0; primary analysis focused on year 2 data with 3,037 youths (ages 11-12 years) from 21 sites with complete sleep and neuroimaging data.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-sectional examination using graph theory and component-based predictive modeling to examine sleep duration associations with brain architecture.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed archival report in Biological Psychiatry: Global Open Science (2025).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Identified phone/SM-specific variable 'screen time' (longer screen times measured as key developmental context) paired with individual-trait neural outcome (somatomotor network disconnection, cognitive performance, externalizing symptoms). Screen time explicitly measured and analyzed as predictor of brain architecture (β = 0.13, p = 2.6 × 10⁻¹⁹).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multivariate predictive modeling using principal component regression; reported multivariate correlations (r = 0.23, p < .001 for sleep-brain relationship; r = 0.15 for dominant component). Linear regressions predicting contextual/phenotypic variables from somatomotor disconnection.

FT_IC4.evidence: Comprehensive numeric results: multivariate correlations (rCV = 0.230, rCV = 0.215), standardized regression coefficients (β = -0.13, p = 2.1 × 10⁻⁹ for somatomotor disconnection and sleep), multiple linear regression β coefficients for context and phenotype associations (e.g., screen time: β = 0.13, p = 2.6 × 10⁻¹⁹).

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; EC7 applies only to longitudinal designs where SM is DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis. This study analyzes screen time as IV predicting brain and behavioral outcomes.
notes: Somatomotor network disconnection represents a novel neural signature of shorter sleep; strongly associated with longer screen times, socioeconomic disadvantage, and reduced cognitive/academic performance. Relationships remained significant after controlling for related contexts and phenotypes.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; analytic sample of 6,554 adolescents with prospectively collected data at baseline (2016-2018, ages 9-10 years), year 1 (2017-2019), and year 2 (2018-2020).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; longitudinal group-based trajectory modeling of social media use over 2 years paired with cognitive performance outcomes assessed at year 2 follow-up.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed letter in JAMA (December 2, 2025; Volume 334, Number 21).

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Identified phone/SM-specific variable 'social media time' (hours per day, measured via group-based trajectory modeling) as IV paired with individual-trait cognitive outcomes (NIH Toolbox Cognition Battery subtests: oral reading recognition, picture sequence memory, picture vocabulary, flanker test, pattern comparison processing speed, composite score). Qualifies as SM variable paired with individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses with robust standard errors examining associations between three trajectory groups (no/very low, low increasing, high increasing social media use) and year 2 cognitive performance across five cognitive subtests and composite score.

FT_IC4.evidence: Comprehensive numeric results reported: adjusted mean differences (95% CI) for all cognitive measures across trajectory groups. Examples: low increasing social media use vs reference: ORRT -1.39 (95% CI, -2.10 to -0.68, p < .001), PSMT -2.03 (95% CI, -2.91 to -1.15, p < .001), composite -0.85 (95% CI, -1.30 to -0.40, p < .001). High increasing: ORRT -1.68 (95% CI, -3.08 to -0.28, p = .02), PSMT -4.51 (95% CI, -6.48 to -2.54, p < .001), composite -1.76 (95% CI, -2.72 to -0.81, p < .001).

FT_EC7_reason: Social media use is IV (predictor) in primary analyses; EC7 requires SM-as-DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere in the paper. This paper explicitly uses SM trajectories to predict cognitive outcomes.
notes: Longitudinal trajectory analysis identifies three distinct patterns of social media use from ages 9-13. Both low and high increasing social media use trajectories significantly associated with lower cognitive performance at year 2, particularly on oral reading recognition, picture sequence memory, and picture vocabulary tests.
